## 

## **1\. Project Overview** {#1.-project-overview}

This project focuses on the intersection of digital wellbeing and mental health. With over 99.8% of Singaporean young adults owning smartphones for communication, study and entertainment (Singstat, 2024), these devices have become integral in their daily routines. However, not all phone uses are equal. While screen time is often treated as a key indicator of problematic smartphone use (PSU) (Tomczyk et al., 2023), other factors such as compulsive checking, emotionally driven use, sleep disruption etc. may be more reflective of PSU. Therefore, this project explores whether these phone-use patterns could be translated into behavioural signals for earlier and lower-friction interventions against PSU.

### **1.1 Background** {#1.1-background}

Mental health burden among young people, particularly university students, remains a growing concern. 

Figure 1: Depressive Disorders Trend by Age Group in Singapore  
Figure 1 illustrates an overall increase in the prevalence of depressive disorders across all age groups in Singapore since 2014\. The upward trend is observed consistently across age groups, indicating a growing mental health burden over time, with the youth group showing highest prevalence throughout, highlighting the importance for earlier support for this group. 

A recent Singapore health and lifestyle study found that problematic smartphone use was strongly associated with poorer mental health outcomes, where respondents in the PSU group are 3.2 times more likely to display moderate or severe depression (Subramaniam et al., 2024).

### 

### **1.2 Motivation**

Figure 2: Relationship between Screen Time and Exposure Pattern with Depression  
Despite rising mental health concerns among Singaporeans, early detection remains a challenge. From Figure 2, our analysis suggests that smartphone usage patterns are associated with higher mental health risk. Notably, individuals with high exposure-pattern intensity exhibit high depression severity regardless of their total screen time. In contrast, individuals with high screen time but low pattern intensity show comparatively lower depression severity. This indicates that total screen time alone is an insufficient indicator of mental health risk, as it fails to capture underlying behavioural patterns such as emotionally driven or context-dependent usage.

However, many existing approaches such as screen time dashboards rely primarily on total screen time, which is overly simplistic and does not account for these behavioural drivers. 

This highlights the need for a data-driven approach that leverages richer behavioural signals to enable more meaningful and early detection of mental health risk, shifting from simple prediction to a system that supports proactive intervention.

### **1.3 Objective** {#1.3-objective}

Our project aims to build a dual-engine system that translates PSU patterns to actionable support. The first engine, a behavioural risk engine produces a PSU risk score together with its main drivers. The second engine, a PHQ-9 validation engine, estimates whether PSU patterns are associated with depressive symptom burden, and produces a binary outcome to predict if an individual is at higher risk of depression. Outputs from both engines are used as signals to guide an intervention tier, for more personalized and low-friction support.

## **2\. Problem Canvas** {#2.-problem-canvas}

### **2.1 Target user** {#2.1-target-user}

University students are the primary target users, characterised by high levels of daily smartphone usage and frequent engagement with digital platforms. Studies indicate that 1 in 2 students spend at least four hours on their phones daily (Shabani et al., 2025), making mobile usage a deeply embedded part of their routines.

This group is particularly relevant as phone use often occurs in contexts linked to vulnerability, such as during studying, when stressed, and before sleep . These patterns suggest that smartphone use is not purely functional but may be driven by emotional and behavioural dependencies. Despite this, students may not recognise when their usage becomes maladaptive and are unlikely to consistently complete traditional mental health screening surveys.

### **2.2 Problem context** {#2.2-problem-context}

In Singapore, 46.4% of youths exhibit problematic smartphone use behaviours (Tushara, 2024), while 1 in 4 young adults report poor mental health , and 1 in 7 experience severe depressive symptoms (IMH, 2024). These trends highlight a growing co-existence of high digital exposure and increasing mental health burden among youths.

While prior research suggests that problematic smartphone use is associated with poorer mental health outcomes, this relationship is not necessarily causal. Nonetheless, the observed association underscores the importance of identifying early behavioural signals that may indicate elevated mental health risk. This highlights a critical need for low-friction methods to detect problematic phone-use patterns at an early stage.

However, these problematic patterns are often difficult to recognise because they are subtle, habitual, and context-dependent rather than purely driven by duration of use. Behaviours such as using the phone to cope with stress, compulsive checking, or usage during inappropriate contexts may not be immediately perceived as harmful by users. As a result, individuals may unknowingly develop maladaptive usage patterns that remain undetected over time.

This issue is further compounded by the tendency of both individuals and existing tools to rely on total screen time as the primary indicator of problematic usage. However, screen time alone does not fully reflect how or why smartphones are used. Consequently, individuals with seemingly moderate usage levels may still exhibit high-risk behavioural patterns. While current solutions such as screen-time dashboards and app timers provide visibility into usage duration, they may not sufficiently surface these underlying behavioural signals, making problematic patterns easy to overlook.

### **2.3 Problem statement** {#2.3-problem-statement}

University students struggle to regulate their smartphone usage habits in today’s digital environment because harmful usage patterns may not always be obvious to users, resulting in undetected increase in depression.

### **2.4 Jobs to Be Done**  {#2.4-jobs-to-be-done}

1. \[**Awareness**\] When a university student uses their phone for extended periods without noticing, I want to be alerted to potentially unhealthy usage patterns, so that I can recognise early signs of problematic behaviour.  
2. \[**Understanding**\] When I am identified as at risk, I want to understand the behavioural reasons behind my usage, so that I can make sense of my habits.  
3. \[**Action**\] When I realise my usage may be unhealthy, I want simple and non-intrusive suggestions, so that I can improve my habits without feeling judged or monitored.

### **2.5 Why current approaches are insufficient** {#2.5-why-current-approaches-are-insufficient}

Current approaches to managing smartphone use include screen time dashboards such as Digital Wellbeing on Android and Screen Time on Apple, application blockers such as AppBlock, and tracking applications such as ActionDash. These tools are effective in providing visibility and control over usage duration.

However, they rely heavily on screen time as the primary indicator of problematic usage and do not capture the underlying behavioural drivers. In particular, they lack insight into behavioural context, such as whether usage is emotionally driven or occurs during inappropriate situations (e.g., studying or before sleep).

This limitation is evident when comparing users with similar or even contrasting levels of screen time. For instance, an individual with high screen time driven by productive activities (e.g., studying or navigation) may exhibit low behavioural risk, while another with moderate screen time characterised by compulsive checking or emotional reliance may exhibit significantly higher risk. This demonstrates that screen time alone is insufficient to differentiate between healthy and problematic usage patterns.

More broadly, existing approaches fall into a digital wellbeing gap, where tools that are low in user burden, such as screen-time dashboards, also tend to have low clinical or behavioural meaningfulness. Conversely, more clinically meaningful approaches, such as repeated mental health surveys, impose higher user burden and are less likely to be adopted consistently. As a result, current solutions fail to provide a balance between meaningful behavioural insight and low-friction user experience.

Furthermore, these tools provide limited guidance beyond reducing usage duration. While they present data or enable restrictions, they do not meaningfully support users in understanding or modifying the behavioural patterns driving their usage. Many of these approaches also require a degree of self-awareness and initiative from users, such as manually setting application limits or blocking apps. However, if problematic usage patterns are difficult to detect in the first place, such features may not be effectively utilised.

Consequently, there is a need for approaches that move beyond duration-based metrics to capture interpretable behavioural signals, enabling more accurate identification of risk and more targeted, low-friction interventions.

### **2.6 Stakeholders** {#2.6-stakeholders}

The primary stakeholders of this project are the end-users of the system, specifically university students. They benefit directly from the early detection and intervention of problematic smartphone usage patterns, enabling them to develop healthier usage habits without feeling judged or monitored.

Secondary stakeholders include mental health professionals or counsellors, as well as application developers. Mental health professionals are involved through a human-in-the-loop design, where high-risk users identified by the system may be guided towards external support services without any clinical diagnosis being made by the system itself. This ensures that appropriate support is provided while maintaining ethical boundaries.

Application developers play a key role in implementing and maintaining the system, working closely with the project team to translate the behavioural models and intervention strategies into a functional and user-friendly application.

## **3\. Data and Exploratory Analysis** {#3.-data-and-exploratory-analysis}

### **3.1 Data sources** {#3.1-data-sources}

Our primary data source is a carefully designed survey tailored to capture behavioural aspects of smartphone usage. While existing online datasets were considered, they were found to be insufficient, as most focus predominantly on aggregate screen time and lack detailed behavioural indicators relevant to this study. As such, a curated survey was necessary to collect more granular data on usage patterns, enabling a more meaningful analysis of behavioural drivers underlying problematic smartphone use.

### **3.2 Data structure** {#3.2-data-structure}

The dataset was constructed from a survey administered to 50 participants aged between 21 and 27, comprising primarily university students (n \= 42), with a small proportion of employed (n \= 7\) and unemployed individuals (n \= 1). Each record in the dataset represents an individual respondent, resulting in a total of 50 observations.

The survey consists of 43 variables capturing multiple aspects of smartphone usage behaviour, along with validated psychological measures. The variables are primarily ordinal and categorical in nature, reflecting frequency-based responses (e.g., Likert-scale items), with some derived numerical features used for analysis.

The survey is structured into five key behavioural domains: exposure intensity, contextual interference, emotional dependence, sleep disruption, and compulsivity. Each domain comprises multiple behavioural items designed to capture different dimensions of smartphone usage patterns, such as frequency of use, usage in inappropriate contexts, emotionally driven usage, and self-regulation behaviours.

In addition to behavioural variables, the dataset includes two target outcomes: the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), which measures depression severity, and the Smartphone Addiction Scale – Short Version (SAS-SV), which provides an overall indicator of problematic smartphone use. These outcomes serve as key reference points for validating the relationship between behavioural patterns and mental health risk.

### **3.3 Why this data is relevant** {#3.3-why-this-data-is-relevant}

The selected behavioural variables are highly relevant as they capture smartphone usage patterns beyond simple duration-based metrics. The five domains \- exposure intensity, contextual interference, emotional dependence, sleep disruption, and compulsivity \- provide interpretable signals that reflect how and why individuals use their smartphones. This aligns directly with the project’s objective of identifying subtle and potentially harmful usage behaviours that may not be evident from screen time alone.

These behavioural features also enable the identification of key drivers behind usage patterns, supporting the design of targeted and actionable interventions.

The inclusion of PHQ-9 and SAS-SV further strengthens the dataset by providing validated measures of depression severity and problematic smartphone use. These outcomes allow the model to assess and validate the relationship between behavioural patterns and mental health risk.

Overall, this combination of behavioural features and validated outcomes ensures that the dataset supports both meaningful risk identification and practical intervention design.

### **3.4 Exploratory data analysis** {#3.4-exploratory-data-analysis}

Figure 3: Distribution of PHQ-9 Score.  
Figure 3 shows a right skewed PHQ-9 distribution, where the majority of students fall into the lower end of the severity scale (minimal to mild), a smaller group with more elevated symptoms and none on the ‘severe’ scale. While there might be sufficient spread to explore and validate our behavioural signals, the class imbalance and absence of ‘severe’ cases should be taken into account in the modeling and evaluation.

Figure 4: Relationship between SAS-SV Score and PHQ-9 Score.  
The scatterplot (Figure 4\) captures the relationship between problematic smartphone use (SAS-SV) and depressive symptoms (PHQ-9). With a correlation of 0.431, a moderate positive relationship between the two, where users who struggle with PSU tend to report higher depressive symptoms. However, the relationship is not deterministic due to the wide spread of data points. 

Despite the limitation, the result remains significant because it provides some evidence that phone habits do carry signals linked to depression, but the moderate link suggests that a single addiction score may be too broad to use as a signal on its own. Hence we decided to look deeper into specific behavioural domains like emotion-driven use or compulsive checking etc.. 

![][image2]  
Figure 5: Relationship between Behavioural Domain with PHQ-9

The scatterplot above (Figure 5\) reveals that all five domains associate positively with PHQ-9 scores, with varying levels of influence. Emotional dependence ([ρ](https://wumbo.net/symbols/rho/) \= 0.388), contextual interference ([ρ](https://wumbo.net/symbols/rho/) \= 0.380), and compulsivity ([ρ](https://wumbo.net/symbols/rho/) \= 0.368) were the strongest predictors. These results indicate that depressive symptoms may be better captured by emotional and function disruption rather than exposure intensity ([ρ](https://wumbo.net/symbols/rho/) \= 0.284) and sleep disruption ([ρ](https://wumbo.net/symbols/rho/) \= 0.188) which showed weaker associations. These 5 domains provide more interpretable signals and directly informs our intervention strategy. 

### **3.5 Key insights from EDA** {#3.5-key-insights-from-eda}

The exploratory analysis provided several key insights that helped shape our final approach. Firstly, the PHQ-9 distribution was skewed towards lower depression severity which led to some data imbalance that have to be cautioned. Secondly, the overall addiction score was too broad to provide intervention insights, hence we looked at behavioural domains, where we discovered domains like emotional dependence and compulsivity were stronger and more meaningful than simple screen time statistics. These results shifted our focus towards the behavioural context of phone use for our modeling and intervention. 

## **4\. Signal to Decision Framework** {#4.-signal-to-decision-framework}

This section outlines the overall decision logic of the system, explaining how raw behavioural data is transformed into interpretable signals that support intervention decisions. In particular, we introduce a dual-signal framework that separates behavioural risk detection from mental health risk estimation, enabling both behavioural intervention and risk validation within a unified pipeline. 

### **4.1 Behavioural signal** {#4.1-behavioural-signal}

The first signal is a behavioural risk score derived from phone-use behaviours, intended to capture patterns such as compulsive checking, emotional reliance on the phone, late-night usage, and difficulties in self-regulation. Instead of using raw survey responses directly, the system first organises behaviours into five structured domains, which represent different aspects of phone-use patterns, including usage intensity, contextual disruption, emotional coping, sleep disruption, and self-regulation difficulty. These domains are then aggregated into a single composite risk index, which is further discretised into low, moderate, and high tiers to support decision-making.  
This signal is important because it provides a direct and interpretable representation of problematic phone-use behaviour, allowing the system to identify risk patterns even without any mental health labels. According to the user-level output structure shown in Appendix 1, users are clearly separated into different behavioural risk tiers while still retaining detailed domain-level profiles and behavioural drivers. Individuals in the high-risk group tend to show consistently elevated scores across multiple behavioural domains, while low-risk users exhibit more moderate and balanced patterns. Each user is also associated with a small set of dominant behavioural drivers, indicating the key dimensions contributing most to their risk profile. This makes the signal both a quantitative ranking mechanism and an interpretable behavioural explanation layer that can support downstream intervention design.

### **4.2 Mental health risk estimation** {#4.2-mental-health-risk-estimation}

The second signal is an estimated risk of elevated depressive symptom burden, which is inferred from behavioural patterns rather than requiring repeated direct mental health screening. Instead of directly diagnosing or replacing clinical assessment, this layer models the statistical association between phone-use behaviour and the likelihood of elevated depressive symptoms. In this framework, behavioural features and aggregated behavioural domains are used to estimate a probability score representing potential mental health risk.  
This signal is important because it adds a secondary interpretive layer that goes beyond behaviour alone, allowing the system to identify whether certain behavioural patterns are not only problematic in usage terms but also potentially associated with broader psychological risk. According to the user-level output structure shown in Appendix 1, each individual is assigned a continuous risk probability alongside their behavioural profile and risk tier. This allows comparison between behavioural severity and estimated mental health risk, where some users may show high behavioural risk but relatively lower estimated depressive risk, while others show alignment across both signals.  
This layer is valuable because it enables risk contextualisation rather than replacement of screening tools such as PHQ-9. In practical settings, repeated mental health screening can be burdensome and may not always be feasible. By leveraging behavioural signals to infer associated risk, the system provides a lightweight complementary mechanism that supports early identification and prioritisation. Importantly, this does not aim to diagnose depression, but to highlight potential risk patterns that may warrant further attention or follow-up assessment.

### **4.3 Why two signals are needed** {#4.3-why-two-signals-are-needed}

The system uses two signals because they capture different but complementary aspects of user risk. The behavioural risk score directly reflects patterns of phone use, such as excessive usage, compulsive checking, emotional reliance, sleep disruption, and difficulties in self-regulation. This signal is focused on describing what the user is doing in terms of behaviour, and it identifies problematic usage patterns even in the absence of any mental health context.  
In contrast, the depressive-risk estimate captures a different layer of information by reflecting the potential association between these behavioural patterns and elevated depressive symptom burden. Rather than describing behaviour itself, it provides a probabilistic indication of whether such behaviour may be linked to broader psychological risk.  
When combined, these two signals allow the system to distinguish between different scenarios. For example, a user may show high behavioural risk due to heavy phone usage but still have a relatively low estimated depressive risk, suggesting primarily behavioural issues without strong mental health implication. Conversely, another user may show both high behavioural risk and high depressive-risk estimation, indicating that the behavioural pattern may be associated with more serious well-being concerns.  
This dual-signal design makes the system more intervention-ready because it supports more nuanced decision-making than either signal alone. It enables separation between behavioural management and potential mental health escalation, allowing interventions to be tailored according to both usage severity and possible psychological relevance.

### **4.4 Decision logic** {#4.4-decision-logic}

The two signals are combined to form a simple intervention logic that maps users into actionable support pathways. The behavioural risk score provides the primary dimension for identifying usage-related concerns, while the estimated depressive risk adds a contextual layer indicating potential psychological relevance.  
Based on these two signals, users can be grouped into different intervention tiers. Users with low behavioural risk and low estimated mental health risk are typically assigned to light-touch interventions, such as reflection prompts or basic usage awareness tools. Users with high behavioural risk but low estimated depressive risk are directed toward self-regulation support, focusing on reducing compulsive or disruptive phone-use patterns. Users with both high behavioural risk and high estimated depressive risk are prioritised for stronger support pathways, which may include more proactive monitoring or escalation to appropriate support resources.  
This structure keeps the decision logic interpretable while ensuring that both behavioural severity and mental health association are reflected in intervention planning.

### **4.5 Intended actionability** {#4.5-intended-actionability}

The outputs of this system are designed to support decision-making rather than serve as standalone classification results. The goal is not only to label users, but to provide structured signals that can directly inform intervention design.  
The behavioural signal enables identification of individuals who may benefit from usage-related behavioural interventions, while the estimated mental health risk helps prioritise cases where additional psychological concern may exist. Together, these outputs allow stakeholders to make more informed and graduated decisions, ranging from light behavioural nudges to stronger forms of support.  
In this sense, the model acts as a decision support tool, translating behavioural data into actionable insights that can be operationalised in real-world intervention settings.

## **5\. Model Prototype** {#5.-model-prototype}

### **5.1 Overview of modelling architecture** {#5.1-overview-of-modelling-architecture}

The modelling prototype consists of two connected engines that operate in sequence to translate raw behavioural data into actionable decision signals. The first engine transforms phone-use behavioural inputs into a structured behavioural risk representation, while the second engine uses these behavioural features to estimate the likelihood of elevated depressive symptom burden. Together, they form a signal-to-intervention pipeline where behavioural patterns are first quantified and then contextualised in terms of potential mental health risk.  
This design reflects a two-layer logic. The first layer focuses on identifying and scoring problematic phone-use behaviour directly, capturing patterns such as excessive usage, emotional reliance, and self-regulation difficulties. The second layer builds on this representation to estimate whether these behavioural patterns are statistically associated with higher depressive risk. In this way, the first engine focuses on behavioural risk profiling, while the second provides a complementary mental health association layer.  
According to the flow chart below, the system structure clearly shows this sequential relationship between behavioural processing and risk estimation. Engine 1 acts as the behavioural feature and scoring module, while Engine 2 operates on the derived behavioural representation to produce a probabilistic estimate of depressive risk. The final output combines both signals into a unified decision-support format, ensuring that usage severity and potential psychological relevance can be interpreted together.  
![][image3]

### 

### **5.2 Engine 1: Behavioural Risk Engine** {#5.2-engine-1:-behavioural-risk-engine}

This engine focuses on transforming raw phone-use survey responses into an interpretable behavioural risk representation. It acts as the first stage of the system, summarising multiple behavioural indicators into structured domains and a final risk score that can support intervention decisions. Unlike predictive modelling, this component emphasises interpretability and behavioural structure rather than outcome prediction. 

#### 

#### **5.2.1 Objective** {#5.2.1-objective}

The objective of this engine is to derive a behavioural risk score and behavioural profile from phone-use survey responses. It aims to capture the severity and pattern of problematic phone-use behaviour in a structured and interpretable form, rather than predicting mental health outcomes directly. 

#### 

#### **5.2.2 Input features** {#5.2.2-input-features}

The input consists of raw phone-use survey items covering multiple behavioural aspects. These are ordinal variables reflecting the frequency and intensity of different usage behaviours, rather than continuous measurements. Instead of treating them independently, the items are grouped into theory-driven behavioural dimensions that represent key constructs of phone-use behaviour.  
These include self-regulation difficulty, sleep disruption, emotional coping behaviour, compulsive checking and usage patterns, and contextual disruption (e.g., during work or meals). These grouped dimensions form the basis for subsequent aggregation into higher-level behavioural constructs used in the risk scoring framework.

#### 

#### **5.2.3 Feature construction logic** {#5.2.3-feature-construction-logic}

Raw survey responses are first standardised to ensure comparability across different Likert scales. Items are then aggregated into behavioural domains through averaging within each conceptual group. Each domain score is normalised to a 0–100 scale to ensure consistent interpretation across dimensions.  
In addition, interaction features between key domains (such as exposure and sleep disruption) are constructed to capture compounded behavioural effects. These engineered features allow the representation to move from individual survey responses to a structured behavioural profile suitable for risk scoring.  
According to the table below, we can observe that the resulting domain-level features exhibit meaningful variability across users, with clear differences in both central tendency and dispersion across behavioural dimensions, indicating that the construction process preserves heterogeneity in behavioural patterns.

|  | mean | std | min | max |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| domain\_exposure\_intensity | 69.527778 | 15.246593 | 37.50 | 94.444444 |
| domain\_context\_disruption | 60.750000 | 14.673313 | 37.50 | 87.500000 |
| domain\_emotional\_coping | 71.833333 | 20.612091 | 0.00 | 100.000000 |
| domain\_sleep\_disruption | 44.500000 | 11.268409 | 6.25 | 62.500000 |
| domain\_self\_regulation | 56.000000 | 20.135595 | 0.00 | 100.000000 |
| psu\_style\_score | 43.480000 | 17.837406 | 14.00 | 90.000000 |

#### 

#### **5.2.4 Modelling / scoring approach** {#5.2.4-modelling-/-scoring-approach}

The behavioural risk score is constructed using a weighted composite framework over the five behavioural domains. Domain weights are derived using a data-driven approach based on principal component analysis, where the first principal component is used to estimate relative importance.  
The final behavioural risk index is computed as a weighted sum of domain scores and then segmented into three tiers (low, moderate, high) using quantile-based thresholds. This ensures that the output is both continuous (for ranking) and discrete (for decision-making).  
According to the domain weights shown in the table below, the contribution of each behavioural domain is relatively balanced, with all weights falling within a narrow range, suggesting that no single behavioural dimension dominates the construction of the overall risk score.

| Domain name | exposure\_intensity | context\_disruption | emotional\_coping | sleep\_disruption | self\_regulation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Weights score** | 0.2039 | 0.1914 | 0.2116 | 0.2032 | 0.1899 |

#### 

#### **5.2.5 Evaluation of Engine 1** {#5.2.5-evaluation-of-engine-1}

Since this engine is a constructed scoring system rather than a predictive classifier, it is evaluated based on interpretability and behavioural consistency rather than traditional classification metrics.  
The behavioural domains combine in a consistent and stable way, and the resulting score reflects expected behavioural patterns: higher exposure, stronger emotional reliance, and weaker self-regulation are associated with higher overall risk scores. The domain contributions remain interpretable after aggregation, meaning each component retains a clear behavioural meaning in the final score.  
In terms of results, the risk tier distribution is relatively balanced, with approximately 17 users in the low group, 16 in the moderate group, and 17 in the high group. This indicates that the scoring system produces a well-distributed segmentation rather than collapsing most users into a single category, supporting its usefulness for downstream intervention grouping.

#### 

#### **5.2.6 Key findings from Engine 1** {#5.2.6-key-findings-from-engine-1}

The analysis shows that multiple behavioural dimensions contribute jointly to risk, rather than a single dominant factor. In particular, emotional coping, exposure intensity, and self-regulation difficulty consistently emerge as strong contributors to higher behavioural risk profiles.  
High-risk users tend to exhibit elevated scores across several domains at the same time, especially in emotional reliance and overall usage intensity. In contrast, low-risk users show more moderate and balanced patterns across all behavioural dimensions, without strong peaks in any single domain.  
Overall, this engine contributes directly to intervention design by providing both a risk ranking and an interpretable explanation of behavioural drivers. An example output of user-level behavioural profiles, including risk tier, domain scores, and top behavioural drivers, is shown in Appendix 1\.

### 

### **5.3 Engine 2: Depressive Risk Estimation Engine** {#5.3-engine-2:-depressive-risk-estimation-engine}

This engine serves as a secondary layer in the overall system, focusing on estimating whether behavioural patterns are associated with elevated depressive symptom burden. Unlike the behavioural engine, which directly models usage patterns, this component provides a contextual risk signal that links behaviour to potential mental health outcomes. It is designed to support screening and prioritisation rather than direct diagnosis. 

#### 

#### **5.3.1 Objective** {#5.3.1-objective}

The objective of this engine is to estimate whether users are likely to exhibit elevated depressive symptom burden based on behavioural patterns. The prediction target is defined using a binary threshold, where PHQ-9 scores below 10 are considered lower risk and scores of 10 or above indicate elevated depressive symptom risk. 

#### 

#### **5.3.2 Input features** {#5.3.2-input-features}

The model primarily uses behavioural features derived from phone-use patterns. In practice, the input includes both detailed behavioural survey items and a small set of app usage indicators, such as social media use, messaging, video streaming, gaming, web browsing, productivity or study apps, and shopping-related usage. These variables are combined into a single feature matrix, and all categorical or boolean variables are converted into numerical form for modelling.  
Missing values are handled using median imputation to ensure model stability, and all features are kept in a consistent numerical format. This setup allows the model to operate on both fine-grained behavioural signals and app-level usage patterns, capturing a broader view of user interaction with mobile devices.

#### 

#### **5.3.3 Data preprocessing** {#5.3.3-data-preprocessing}

The dataset is first split into training and testing subsets using a stratified approach to preserve class distribution. Categorical or boolean variables are encoded into numerical format where necessary, and all missing values are handled using median imputation. Feature scaling is applied for models sensitive to feature magnitude, such as logistic regression. Given the imbalance in the target distribution, evaluation explicitly accounts for class imbalance through appropriate metrics rather than relying on accuracy alone. 

#### 

#### **5.3.4 Models considered** {#5.3.4-models-considered}

Several baseline models are evaluated to assess the relationship between behavioural features and depressive risk. These include logistic regression as a linear baseline, random forest as a non-linear ensemble method, and gradient boosting as a more flexible tree-based model. The purpose of this comparison is not to optimise a single model aggressively, but to assess whether a consistent signal exists across different modelling assumptions. 

#### 

#### **5.3.5 Evaluation approach** {#5.3.5-evaluation-approach}

Model performance is assessed using multiple metrics that are appropriate for imbalanced classification tasks. These include ROC-AUC, precision, recall, F1-score, and balanced accuracy. A confusion matrix is also used to interpret error types.  
In this context, recall is particularly important because failing to identify users with elevated depressive risk is more problematic than generating false positives. As a result, models that improve recall are prioritised even if precision is moderate.

#### 

#### **5.3.6 Results** {#5.3.6-results}

Overall, the models show limited but non-trivial predictive signal. Logistic regression performs as the most stable baseline, while more complex tree-based models do not consistently improve detection of positive cases. The results indicate that behavioural features contain some signal related to depressive risk, but the strength of this association is moderate rather than strong.  
In practical terms, the model functions better as a screening support tool rather than a precise classifier. It is more reliable for flagging potential risk cases than for confirming low-risk status. False positives are acceptable in this context, as they primarily lead to additional review, while false negatives represent missed potential risk cases and are therefore more critical.

#### 

#### **5.3.7 Model interpretation** {#5.3.7-model-interpretation}

The most influential behavioural signals in the model are generally consistent with theory-driven expectations. These include emotional coping behaviour, sleep disruption, self-regulation difficulty, late-night phone usage, and compulsive checking patterns. These factors tend to be more strongly associated with elevated depressive symptom risk compared to other behavioural dimensions.  
Overall, the model relies more heavily on affective and regulation-related behaviours rather than purely usage intensity, indicating that emotional and behavioural regulation patterns carry more relevance for depressive risk estimation than general phone usage volume.

### 

### **5.4 Why both engines are needed** {#5.4-why-both-engines-are-needed}

The two engines serve distinct but complementary roles within the system. Engine 1 captures behavioural harm directly by modelling problematic phone-use patterns such as excessive engagement, emotional reliance, sleep disruption, and self-regulation difficulty. This allows the system to identify users with potentially unhealthy usage behaviours even when there is no clear indication of elevated depressive symptom risk.  
Engine 2 adds a second layer by estimating whether these behavioural patterns are associated with a higher likelihood of depressive symptom burden. It does not replace the behavioural signal, but instead provides contextual information about potential mental health relevance.  
Together, the two engines enable more nuanced intervention decisions than either model alone. Engine 1 identifies what is happening behaviourally, while Engine 2 provides insight into why it may matter clinically. This separation is intentional, because the goal of the system is not only to predict PHQ-9 outcomes, but to detect problematic phone-use behaviour and integrate it into an intervention-oriented decision pipeline.

### 

### **5.5 Limitations across both engines** {#5.5-limitations-across-both-engines}

There are several limitations that apply across both engines. A key constraint is that the entire framework relies on self-reported survey responses, which may not fully reflect actual passive or long-term phone-use behaviour. As a result, both behavioural scoring and downstream risk estimation are influenced by subjective reporting bias, which can introduce noise into the signals.  
For Engine 2 in particular, the model captures statistical association between behavioural patterns and elevated depressive symptom risk, but it does not imply causation. In addition, the use of a binary PHQ-9 threshold simplifies mental health into a coarse classification, which may not fully reflect the underlying spectrum of psychological states. The observed performance should therefore be interpreted as supporting triage rather than providing diagnostic-level accuracy.  
Finally, both engines are developed and evaluated within a controlled dataset setting, and their generalisation to real-world deployment requires further validation. This includes testing on more diverse populations and longitudinal settings to ensure stability and robustness of both behavioural scoring and risk estimation.

## 

## **6\. Product Interface and Intervention Design** {#6.-product-interface-and-intervention-design}

This section describes how our model and intervention techniques will be integrated into a usable solution.

### 

### **6.1 Product concept** {#6.1-product-concept}

Our solution is a digital mental wellness tool designed as a mobile-based intervention layer that translates behavioural data into actionable insights.  
The product focuses on helping users reflect on their daily habits and identify early signs of mental strain through non-clinical, easy-to-understand feedback. Rather than functioning as a diagnostic tool, it acts as a supportive guide, offering timely nudges and personalised suggestions.

### 

### **6.2 Core product functions** {#6.2-core-product-functions}

The product collects behavioural inputs, such as phone usage patterns, activity levels and inferred behavioural insights. Using model outputs to summarise user patterns into interpretable indicators, it will generate a behavioural risk profile and estimate potential mental health risk. This will be framed in non-clinical terms, *higher stress patterns observed*, for example. Based on the severity and persistence of behavioural signals, an intervention tier is assigned. Lastly, it presents simple, personalised and actionable recommendations.  
This pipeline ensures that insights move from raw data to meaningful intervention in a user-friendly way.

### 

### **6.3 Intervention tiers** {#6.3-intervention-tiers}

The system adopts a tiered intervention approach, aligned with model outputs:

| Low Risk | Users receive gentle nudges and light-touch reminders. The focus is on increasing awareness and maintaining healthy habits. |
| :---- | :---- |
| Moderate Risk | Users are encouraged to perform self check-ins and engage in guided reflection. The focus is on identifying negative trends and supporting gradual behavioural change. |
| High Risk | Users are signposted to external support resources, such as mental health platforms or community services. At this level, the system prioritises help-seeking behaviour without making clinical judgements. |

This structured escalation ensures that interventions remain proportionate, supportive and responsible.

### 

### **6.4 Targeted interventions** {#6.4-targeted-interventions}

Interventions are tailored based on key behavioural drivers identified by the model, delivering practical and personalised recommendations that users can easily act on.

| Self-regulation | App usage limits, friction design and goal-setting features |
| :---- | :---- |
| Sleep disruption | Bedtime nudges, night mode prompts and sleep hygiene suggestions |
| Emotional coping patterns | Mood check-ins, journaling prompts, breathing exercises, and coping strategies. |

### 

### **6.5 Interface prototype** {#6.5-interface-prototype}

Our prototype is designed as a supportive, non-clinical mobile interface that helps users understand their smartphone behaviour, reflect on emerging risk patterns, and receive proportionate interventions. The interface is intentionally calm, simple, and non-judgemental, so that users feel guided rather than evaluated.

#### 

#### 

#### Risk Summary {#risk-summary}

The main insight screen presents a weekly behavioural summary in a clear and digestible way. Rather than showing a harsh score or diagnostic label, the interface explains the user’s current state through simple visual summaries and key drivers, such as stress-related use, boredom, or habit. This allows the system to communicate risk in a more interpretable and less threatening manner. This helps users understand why a certain intervention may be suggested without feeling judged.

#### 

#### Intervention Recommendations {#intervention-recommendations}

The next prototype screen translates insight into one recommended next step. For example, after identifying a possible late-night or stress-related pattern, the app suggests a simple and achievable action such as taking a five-minute offline breath after dinner. Instead of overwhelming users with many recommendations, the interface presents a single actionable suggestion that feels realistic and low-stakes. This increases the likelihood of compliance and makes the output feel supportive rather than prescriptive.

#### 

#### Low-risk Users {#low-risk-users}

The prototype focuses on gentle nudges and early habit support. The first screen provides simple phone-free activities that are light-touch and preventive, reflecting the idea that low-risk users do not need strong intervention, but may still benefit from small behavioural changes.

#### 

#### 

#### 

#### Moderate-risk Users {#moderate-risk-users}

The interface introduces more reflective and emotionally aware intervention. The app encourages a short self-check-in by asking how the user has been feeling and prompting reflection on their current mood or energy level. This is appropriate for users showing repeated negative patterns but not yet requiring escalation. The design here shifts from simple habit nudges to well-being reflection, which matches the increased behavioural concern while remaining non-clinical and supportive.

#### High-risk Users {#high-risk-users}

The prototype introduces a human-in-the-loop support pathway. The app signposts users to external resources such as mindline.sg and other local support services. Importantly, these screens avoid diagnostic labels and instead frame support as optional, caring, and accessible. The interface uses language such as “support,” “resource,” and “speak with a guide,” rather than clinical conclusions. This is a conscious design decision, where higher-risk outputs should not lead to purely automated judgement, so the interface escalates users toward human or professional support in a careful and non-stigmatising way.

## 

## **7\. Safety, Ethics, and Human-in-the-Loop** {#7.-safety,-ethics,-and-human-in-the-loop}

With sensitive data on mental health involved, good safety protocols are paramount. Mental health interventions are sensitive for the following reasons: 

1. Users may be vulnerable  
2. False reassurance can be harmful to users  
3. Over-escalation may cause unnecessary distress  
4. Algorithmic outputs cannot fully replace professional judgement

The product is intended to identify behavioural patterns associated with problematic smartphone use and provide proportionate, low-stakes interventions such as usage insights, coping nudges, or signposting to support resources. It is **not** intended to diagnose addiction, depression, or any mental health condition, and it should **not** be used to make high-stakes clinical decisions independently. This distinction is important to reduce the risk of over-trusting the system and to ensure that automated outputs are interpreted cautiously.

### 

### **7.1 Clinical Risks and Potential Harm** {#7.1-clinical-risks-and-potential-harm}

#### False Positives {#false-positives}

These occur when the model incorrectly classifies a user as being at elevated risk of problematic smartphone use.This may lead to overly restrictive recommendations, or concerns being raised when there is no meaningful problem that exists. Beyond reduced user trust, repeated false alarms may cause frustration and lack of trust in the product. To mitigate this, interventions for medium-risk outputs should remain low-stakes, non-punitive, and easy to dismiss or customise.

#### False Negatives {#false-negatives}

The second risk is false negatives, where a high-risk user is not identified. This is particularly concerning because the users who require the interventions the most are not receiving the help that they need. As a result, the system may provide insufficient intervention or fail to escalate users toward more appropriate mental health resources. In practice, false negatives may be more harmful than false positives as they create a false sense of reassurance while leaving higher-risk users unsupported. To mitigate this risk, the system should prioritise sensitivity for higher-risk cases where possible, monitor repeated patterns over time rather than relying on one-off predictions, and use conservative escalation logic for persistent elevated-risk signals.

#### Overdependence on App-Based Scoring {#overdependence-on-app-based-scoring}

Another concern is that users may place excessive confidence in an app’s risk score. Behavioural risk scores are only proxies derived from limited observable signals, and human judgement, clinical assessment, or contextual understanding should not be fully replaced by these scores. To mitigate this, the system should avoid making strong conclusions from single behavioural indicators, rely on multiple signals where possible, and frame outputs as probabilistic rather than definitive. Outlier or ambiguous cases should receive neutral, low-stakes recommendations rather than strong escalation.

### **7.2 Human-in-the-loop design** {#7.2-human-in-the-loop-design}

Given the sensitivity of such behavioural risk prediction, simply relying on an automated system would be inappropriate for higher-stakes decisions. A human-in-the-loop design is therefore important to ensure that the model outputs are interpreted cautiously, acted on responsibly, and communicated in a way that supports rather than harms users.

Firstly, referral related recommendations should be framed carefully. If the system suggests resources such as mindline.sg, these recommendations should not appear as diagnostic. Instead, they should be presented as supported resources that are helpful. This reduces the risk of stigma, panic, or inappropriate self-labelling. Human review is particularly valuable in designing and refining the wording of these recommendations so that they are empathetic, non-judgemental, and proportionate to the level of risk.

Next, users should be encouraged to seek professional support when necessary. As mentioned previously, when users are deemed as high-risk, they are then referred to mindline.sg, which allows for them to seek professional care. The purpose of this system is not to replace trained professionals, but to support early awareness and intervention. In such cases, the system should move from lightweight nudges toward signposting external support, while making clear that the app is not a replacement for professional care.

Overall, human oversight should be embedded at points where model outputs may influence higher-stakes recommendations, especially for elevated-risk users. This helps ensure that the system remains supportive, context-sensitive, and ethically responsible, while preserving the role of professional judgement in matters related to mental well-being.

### **7.3 Privacy and consent** {#7.3-privacy-and-consent}

This product relies on sensitive data, and as such, informed consent should be required. Users are clearly told what data is being collected, why it is needed, and how it will be used, and what kinds of intervention may result. Data collection should also follow the principle of minimization, meaning that only signals necessary for the intended purpose are gathered, rather than broad or invasive monitoring. As such, privacy and consent must be treated as core design requirements.

In addition, safe storage and handling of sensitive data should be emphasised. This could be through methods such as appropriate storage and protection against malicious attacks. Users should also be able to retain control over their participation, including the ability to opt in and out, or limit certain forms of monitoring. Overall, privacy-preserving design is essential to ensure that the system remains trustworthy.

### **7.4 Appropriate scope of the system** {#7.4-appropriate-scope-of-the-system}

The purpose of the product is to identify behavioural patterns associated with problematic smartphone use and deliver supportive, proportionate interventions such as awareness nudges, coping suggestions, and signposting to relevant resources.

Clear boundaries around the product’s scope are essential to reduce over-reliance, protect users from inappropriate labelling, and ensure that professional judgement remains central in cases involving higher-risk mental health concerns.

### **7.5 Red team scenario analysis** {#7.5-red-team-scenario-analysis}

A red team exercise helps identify how the system may fail in realistic but harmful edge cases. For example, a user with heavy phone use due to exams, caregiving, or shift work may be wrongly flagged as high-risk, leading to unnecessary escalation. Conversely, a genuinely vulnerable user may be missed if their behaviour does not match the model’s learned patterns. Another failure mode is that recommendation wording may become stigmatising or overly certain, especially if generated automatically.

These scenarios highlight the need for safeguards such as low-stakes default interventions, careful handling of ambiguous cases, human oversight for higher-risk outputs, and tightly controlled wording for referral-related recommendations.

## **8\. Evaluation Framework** {#8.-evaluation-framework}

To evaluate the effectiveness of our solution, we must consider model-level and product-level metrics, alongside safety and ethical considerations.

### **8.1 Model-level evaluation** {#8.1-model-level-evaluation}

At the model level, we assess how well the system identifies users with elevated behavioural risk. Key evaluation metrics include:

* Recall measures the proportion of truly at-risk users correctly identified. This is particularly important to minimise false negatives (missed cases).  
* Precision measures how many flagged users are actually at risk. This helps reduce false positives (excessive judging).  
* Receiver Operating Characteristic \- Area Under Curve (ROC-AUC) evaluates the model’s overall ability to distinguish between risk levels across different thresholds.

In our application, false positives result in unnecessary interventions but false negatives result in elevated-risk users not being identified. We would penalise the latter.

### **8.2 Intervention-level evaluation** {#8.2-intervention-level-evaluation}

Beyond model performance, it is important to assess whether the product is useful in practice, leading to real behavioural and well-being outcomes.  
Key outcomes include:

* Reduction in unhealthy phone-use patterns, such as excessive late-night usage.  
* Improved sleep-related habits, through more consistent sleep timing.  
* Improved emotional regulation through the use of coping strategies like journaling or breathing exercises  
* User retention and return rates

These metrics reflect whether the intervention is actually creating meaningful impact in users’ daily lives.

### **8.3 Future validation** {#8.3-future-validation}

To strengthen the robustness and real-world applicability of our solution, several next steps are required:

* Collection of larger and more diverse datasets to allow the model to learn more behavioural patterns and generalise better across different populations.  
* Integration of passive behavioural signals such as screen unlocks, app usage patterns and temporal usage data, to capture behaviour more accurately and timely without relying on self-reported data.  
* Longitudinal evaluation, by tracking users over time to assess sustained behavioural and well-being outcomes, can assess whether intervention effects persist over time.

These steps will move our proof-of-concept system to a validated real-world intervention tool.

## **9\. Learning and Reflections** {#9.-learning-and-reflections}

### **9.1 What the team learned** {#9.1-what-the-team-learned}

One of the biggest lessons we took away from this project was that mental health goes far beyond simple prediction. Initially we focused on predicting PHQ-9 risk through smartphone variables. But as we explored the survey results, we found more value in figuring out how to translate those phone-use patterns into support that actually makes sense to a user. That was what led to our dual-engine framework, where first, the behavioural risk engine focuses on capturing problematic behaviours directly, while second, the PHQ-9 engine provides the necessary context on whether those patterns align with an elevated risk of depression.

Our team also learned that working in a mental health context requires a greater level of caution compared to typical data science problems. On top of identifying signals, we also had to prioritize preventing harm, where users might feel judged, monitored, or the system might overstep its bounds and appear to diagnose users. Hence we decided to include human-in-the-loop escalation, and positioned our second engine as a supportive check rather than a primary driver of our system. 

### **9.2 What worked well** {#9.2-what-worked-well}

The project benefited significantly from a grounded problem statement. We focused on a prevalent and relatable issue even amongst users other than university students, the issue of phone use during stressful periods, whilst studying and right before bed. These are moments where habits can easily slip from “normal” to “problematic” without user noticing. By honing in on these specific scenarios, we were able to identify university students as a primary target user and include these scenarios for investigation into our survey as one of our features to allow for targeted intervention. 

Secondly, even though the number of survey respondents was limited relative to the number of available features, grouping the features into behavioural domains allowed us to reduce the input space without compromising important signals, which helped keep the model from overfitting to a small sample and greatly improving recall and F1 score. Furthermore, focusing on behavioural domains allowed us to look beyond just screen-time numbers, while also improving interpretability of our models and providing a clear connection between specific types of phone use and the resulting risk levels. 

### **9.3 What could be improved** {#9.3-what-could-be-improved}

Our most important limitation is our dataset. Firstly, our survey only had 50 respondents and are mainly concentrated on university students. This affects the generalisability of the findings and prevents the exploration of other prediction models such as random forest or historical gradient boosting which requires more data. Secondly, our survey relied on self-reporting rather than passive sensing, which introduces the risk of recall bias as participants might not always remember or report their phone habits with perfect accuracy. Moving forward, we should focus on collecting a wider, more diverse range of data, and shift towards collecting data through passive sensing wherever possible to avoid inconsistencies of self-reporting.

Another limitation is our engines. While our behavioural risk engine is easy to interpret, it is still sensitive to our weighting and domain choices, which require further validation. Next, we have to be careful how we interpret the PHQ-9 validation engine. Despite logistic regression performing the best with recall being prioritized, it only works well as a supporting tool, and should not be misinterpreted as a standalone screener. 

Lastly, we need to see how this intervention actually holds up in the real world. While the prototype and user flow looks good on paper, we assumed that users will be receptive to the nudges and check-ins, and if it would actually change their behaviour. It is also worth looking closer at fairness across different groups and finding a better way to differentiate between someone who is using their phone for work versus someone who is harmfully addicted to their phone. 

## **10\. Conclusion** {#10.-conclusion}

The use of smartphones is so deeply embedded in university students’ lives that it is difficult to tell when those habits become a problem. PSU behaviours like phone use to cope with stress or letting it interfere with sleep tend to develop slowly and are easily missed by current screen-time trackers. Furthermore, repeated mental health screening or surveys may be too high-friction and burdensome for students to complete. Therefore emphasising the need for a way to identify these behaviours early before it exacerbates. 

This project utilises a dual-engine framework to make sense of PSU. Engine 1 derives a risk score and its drivers, while engine 2 evaluates whether PSU behaviours are linked to depressive symptoms, and produces a predicted depressive outcome. However its purpose is not diagnosis, but rather a secondary layer of validation that supports how we prioritize interventions. Together, these engines allow for a tiered intervention strategy that will be available in our prototype that focuses on non-clinical, personalized support.

Overall, this project proves that digital wellbeing tools should focus on behaviours and reasons behind the screen rather than just its raw duration. Its significance comes beyond just its prediction capability, but how it connects sensing, data interpretation and safety guards into a single framework. While the system is still in its early stages, where more testing and improvements can be made to validate its effectiveness, it provides a promising roadmap for using data to design more personalized interventions for university students.

## **References** {#references}

IMH. (2024). Excessive social media use, body image concerns and being cyberbullied are significantly linked to mental health symptoms among youths. *Institute of Mental Health.* [https://www.imh.com.sg/Newsroom/News-Releases/Documents/NYMHS\_Press%20Release\_FINAL19Sep2024.pdf](https://www.imh.com.sg/Newsroom/News-Releases/Documents/NYMHS_Press%20Release_FINAL19Sep2024.pdf)  
Shabani, Z., Haxhija, E., Pjetri E., Guli, A. (2025). Smartphone Addiction among University Students. *The Open Public Health Journal* [https://doi.org/10.2174/0118749445414366250828054906](https://doi.org/10.2174/0118749445414366250828054906)  
Singstat. (2024). Individuals Smartphone Ownership By Age Group, Annual. *Singapore Department of Statistics* [https://data.gov.sg/datasets/d\_65567444c3df02aceb795897bbd183c9/view](https://data.gov.sg/datasets/d_65567444c3df02aceb795897bbd183c9/view)  
Subramaniam, M., Koh, Y. S., Sambasivam, R., Samari, E., Abdin, E., Jeyagurunathan, A., Tan, B. C. W., Zhang, Y., Ma, S., Chow, W. L., & Chong, S. A. (2024). Problematic smartphone use and mental health outcomes among Singapore residents: The health and lifestyle survey. *Asian Journal of Psychiatry, 98*, 104124\. [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ajp.2024.104124](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ajp.2024.104124)  
Tomczyk, L., Lizde, E.S.. (2023). Is real screen time a determinant of problematic smartphone and social network use among young people? *Telematics and Informatics*. [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tele.2023.101994.](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tele.2023.101994)  
Tushara, E. (2024). About one in two S’porean youth has problematic smartphone use: IMH study. *The Straits Times* [https://www.straitstimes.com/singapore/about-one-in-two-s-porean-youth-has-problematic-smartphone-use-imh-study](https://www.straitstimes.com/singapore/about-one-in-two-s-porean-youth-has-problematic-smartphone-use-imh-study)
